data.title: QQQI: The New Class Of The Nasdaq 100 High Income ETFs
data.description:
High yield derivative income ETFs have become all the rage over the past 18 months. Unfortunately, most of the attention is going towards the single stock (and, in some cases, leveraged single stock) covered call ETFs, which can sometimes offer annualized distribution yields of 100% or more.

But the old fashioned covered call ETFs that are based on a broad index and aim to deliver a high yield without going over the top are still pretty good too.

For years, the standard bearer for covered call strategies based on the Nasdaq 100 has been the Global X Nasdaq 100 Covered Call ETF (QYLD). Its strategy is sublimely simple - 1) own the Nasdaq 100, 2) write an at-the-money call option with a one-month expiration, 3) collect the premium, 4) repeat the process by selling a new call option in the following month.

Using this process, QYLD has done a terrific job of generating steady and predictable monthly income for shareholders. Over the past three years, monthly distributions have varied within a range of only about $0.17 to $0.20 per share, rising when volatility ticks up and vice versa.

Its strategy of distributing half of all option income premiums received up to a monthly cap of 1% has produced a very steady annualized distribution yield of around 12%.

In short, it's been a very durable high income generating machine!

But as is often the case with products that proven successful, competitors emerge. Today, there are about a half dozen ETFs that produce a similar strategy to QYLD based on the Nasdaq 100.

One competitor, however, has emerged from the pack - the NEOS Nasdaq 100 High Income ETF (NASDAQ:QQQI).

How Does QQQI Work?

QQQI is an actively-managed fund that seeks to generate high monthly income in a tax efficient manner while maintaining the potential for some share price appreciation.

It plans to distribute monthly income by investing in the constituents of the Nasdaq 100 while simultaneously implementing a data-driven call option strategy. This strategy can involve both selling and purchasing NDX index options in order to adjust the income/capital growth profile of the fund.

The key feature of any good actively-managed strategy is the ability to outperform the returns you might get from a standard index-benchmarked ETF. Just being actively-managed doesn't create opportunities in a vacuum. The fund's investment strategy needs to come with a differentiator that sets it up to produce superior risk-adjusted returns.

I feel that QQQI does that.

The "data-driven" part of the strategy is a bit of a black box that we won't have a full understanding of, but the quant-focused approach has so far done a pretty good job of positioning the fund correctly in order to capture optimal income/capital growth from the strategy.

This video from NEOS explains how QQQI operates.

Comparing QQQI to QYLD

If there's one drawback to QYLD, it's that it's inflexible. Tracking an index, the fund is unable to shift in response to market conditions. It's use of at-the-money call options may help maximize the yield the fund can generate, but it also caps most share price upside.

QQQI aims to address some of those issues. Its dynamic overlay strategy allows the managers the ability to add or reduce exposure depending on what the market is doing while maintaining that high yield. The use of out-of-the-money call options may potentially limit some income capture, but it also opens the door for capital growth. That's been an important feature over the past couple years as the Nasdaq 100 has been moving higher in nearly a straight line.

Here's a summary of how QYLD lines up with QQQI.

Global X NASDAQ 100 Covered Call ETF (QYLD) NEOS Nasdaq 100 High Income ETF (QQQI)
Issuer Global X NEOS
Launch Date December 11, 2013 January 30, 2024
Management Style Passive Active
Index CBOE Nasdaq-100 BuyWrite V2 Index none
Primary Strategy Holds Nasdaq 100 stocks and sells covered calls on the index Holds Nasdaq 100 stocks and both buys & sells covered calls on the index
Option Term One-month expiration One-month expiration
Option Moneyness At-the-money Out-of-the-money
Overlay Strategy 100% overlay at all times Dynamic
Objective High monthly income

High monthly income
Upside Potential Generally limited Some depending on credit spread
Expense Ratio 0.60% 0.68%
Assets Under Management (as of 10/20/25) $8.1 billion $5.8 billion
Distribution Frequency Monthly Monthly
Distribution Yield (ttm) (as of 9/30/25) ~13.6% ~14.3%
Tax Treatment Distributions often include return of capital; may be less tax-efficient Uses Section 1256 contracts (60% LT / 40% ST) for enhanced tax efficiency

A couple things to note:

QQQI doesn't explicitly post what its option overlay percentage is at any given point, so it's tougher to know where the fund is being positioned especially bullish or bearish.

We do, however, know that looking at the fund's latest holdings shows a short NDX call with a strike of 25,200 and another short NDX call with a strike of 25,600, both with expirations in late November. There's no long call listed, so we can probably assume that QQQI is positioned relatively neutrally right now.

Also, the tax treatment is worth mentioning. QQQI's use of Section 1256 contracts means that distributions have a more defined tax treatment that can prove advantageous in certain situations. Of QYLD's fiscal year distributions, more than 98% are characterized as return of capital. That can defer tax liabilities, but it also erodes the cost basis.

A lot of investors don't really consider the tax consequences of their investments. QYLD may or may not produce a better tax result based on your individual situation, but QQQI gives you more of a known quantity (and one structured to likely be better by comparison).

The yields are also interesting to note. QQQI has consistently produced a higher distribution yield than QYLD over its brief history. That means that shareholders are getting an income boost plus the potential for additional capital gains.

QQQI Performance Recap

QQQI and QYLD should never be expected to compete with QQQ in a rising market based on their structures. QQQI, however, has come pretty close on a total return basis.

QQQI likely layered some long call positions over the portfolio at least during parts of the AI boom. QYLD's returns have been steadier, but more in line with what we'd expect to see in a rising market - significantly lagging total returns.

QQQI may be more vulnerable during bear markets and corrections given its higher historical volatility. Over the long-term, however, that could play out in its favor given how stocks tend to rise more often than not.

Conclusion

For those seeking more of a pure income play, QYLD might be the better choice given its lower volatility profile, steadier returns and predictable income. There's certainly nothing wrong with that play if you're that kind of investor.

Overall, however, QQQI is my preference. Its growth tilt and active management have proven to be effective in positioning the fund in the right ways at the right time. The flexibility of adding to or reducing exposure is a key advantage if the management team can play it right. It's a short history, but it's worked so far.

If QQQI can maintain its distribution yield advantage too, it might prove to be the better choice for a wider group of income investors.

data.title: Ford Earnings: A Sigh Of Relief, But Not Enough To Justify A Buy Rating
data.description:
During the Q3 2025 earnings release of Ford Motor Company (NYSE:F) the main focus seems to have fallen on the impact of the fire at the aluminum supplier Novelis and the net tariff headwind.

These two developments have been over lingering on investors' minds in recent months, with the latter being responsible for the guidance suspension earlier this year.

The former is a more recent event and it did cause F share price to lag those of its peers in recent weeks. Ford is one of the biggest losers from the fire at the Novelis factory as it provides material for the production of the iconic F-150 truck.

Nonetheless, the market now seems relieved after Ford's management provided a revised outlook for FY 2025, alongside ranges for certain cost items that will continue to weigh on profitability into FY 2026 as well. Although the updated guidance (see the table below) is notably lower to the one provided in February of this year, the market was already anticipating that which is causing Ford share price to be up by low single digits following the release.

In addition to the $1bn net tariff impact in 2025, Ford would experience an EBIT headwind of $1.5bn to $2bn this year. However, the management expects to mitigate roughly $1bn in the following year.

Between 2025 and 2026, we expect Novelis to be a headwind of $1 billion or less. For 2025, we expect an adjusted EBIT headwind of $1.5 billion to $2 billion in the fourth quarter for Novelis, and we currently have line of sight to mitigate at least $1 billion in 2026, and we are working to improve the situation further.

Source: Ford Q3 2025 Earnings Transcript

All in all, the market was not caught off-guard by any of that new information and as a result F share price is not materially changed and continues to lag behind most of its peers, with the exception of Stellantis (STLA).

While tariffs and the fire make for very interesting and dramatic headlines, long-term investors do not need to pay much attention to these issues as they are already being priced-in and there's also significant uncertainty when forecasting the overall impact of trade tensions.

Operational Performance

When it comes to operational performance, there was nothing noteworthy during the third quarter of 2025 which further supports the somewhat muted share price reaction in the early hours. Moving beyond the more immediate impacts on Ford's business, however, the quarterly results confirmed some ongoing trends that I find unsettling.

As a starting point, Ford's management has once again stressed the fact that in-spite of the current tariff headwinds they should level the playing field within medium and heavy-duty trucks - the high margin segments for the company. According to the CEO, Ford has been put at a disadvantage by producing these vehicles in the United States and tariffs would now address the issue of more competitive imports.

The overall impact of this is still unclear and in the meantime Ford's automotive margin continues to decline.

On a segmented basis, there are two bright spots for the company - Ford Pro and Ford Credit, with the former bringing significantly higher profits. The good news is that lower spend in the government sub-segment of Ford Pro has been offset by strength in small to medium businesses. As a result, profits in Ford Pro segment reached $2bn in the third quarter.

Ford Blue - the internal combustion engine segment for retail customers, however, continues on the recent downtrend with operating profit continuously falling in each of the Q3 periods shown above. The EV segment was gradually moving towards breaking-even, but the most recent report has shown otherwise with losses now exceeding those in Q3 2023. On top of all that, Ford's management did not sound very optimistic as far as achieving profitability in the segment is concerned.

Year-to-date, Model e is at a $3.6 billion loss. Roughly $3 billion of this is from our first-generation products, Mach-E, Lightning, Puma, Explore and Capri. The balance is investment in our next-generation vehicles, including our UEB platform. The only practical way to improve the profitability of our Gen 1 vehicles is through one of the more of the following: pricing, new cost reductions and improved fixed cost leverage. Given current industry trends, it's clear scaling fixed costs is a challenge for most of the industry.

Source: Ford Q3 2025 Earnings Transcript

The last positive area of the business was Ford Credit, where improved financing margins contributed to yet another strong quarter. Profit improved significantly over the past 12 month when compared to FY 2024, but still remains below 2022.

The trend of rising auction values, however, seems to have come to an end during the last quarter and loss-to-receivables ratio has jumped to Q1 2025 highs. This is by no means a trend-setting event, but is something that investors should follow closely.

Conclusion

The Q3 2025 report of Ford gave investors a sigh of relief after all the uncertainty around one-off events the ongoing trade tensions. That is why, Ford share price might continue its ascend in the coming weeks and even months, provided there are no adverse events within the market. Having said that, however, the market was accurately pricing-in these recent headwinds and investors should not rely in the quarterly results as a reason to buy. Moreover, from operational point of view, the quarter presented more challenges that opportunities for investors.

data.title: Realty Income: My Top Pick For Income Investors (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Performance assessment

Realty Income Corporation (NYSE:O) has slightly lagged the broader market index since my last update on the stock:

I think my 'Market Perform/Hold' rating has been accurate.

Thesis

Now, I'm becoming more bullish on O. Here's why:

  1. Realty Income is set to expand its portfolio at attractive spreads
  2. The ingredients are in place to support higher investment activity as well
  3. Lower interest rates are a tailwind for the stock
  4. O stock is attractively valued at a discount to its comps and may be poised for a multiple re-rating
  5. The technicals are turning bullish
  6. Management's expectations reflect higher tenant credit risks

Realty Income is set to expand its portfolio at attractive spreads

Realty Income is finding ample opportunities to expand its portfolio in Europe this year:

The size of the opportunity in Europe is currently larger than that in the US with the added benefit of lower borrowing costs as well:

We again leaned into Europe which accounted for $889 million or 76% of our investment volume at a 7.3% weighted average initial cash yield. Europe remains a compelling growth market driven by a fragmented competitive landscape, a larger total addressable market than what is available in the United States and a cost of debt capital that is currently more favorable with euro borrowing costs approximately of 120 basis points inside of U.S. dollar debt costs for 10-year notes as of today.

- CEO Sumit Roy in the Q2 FY25 earnings call

Since that Q2 FY25 earnings call on 6 Aug'25, the Federal Reserve has has cut rates by 25bps, whilst the ECB has kept rates steady. Hence, I estimate that the EU cost of debt capital is still cheaper than the US, but by a smaller margin of close to 100bps rather than 120bps. The bottom line is that it is still very advantageous for Realty Income to expand in Europe. And my next point discusses why I expect this to continue:

The ingredients are in place to support higher investment activity as well

Realty Income is upgrading its investment guidance from $4 billion to $5 billion for FY25 along with their per-share AFFO guidance:

We deployed $1.2 billion into investments during the second quarter at an initial weighted average cash yield of 7.2%. These results were bolstered by our growing presence in Europe, which accounted for 76% of our investment volume. Supported by consistent first half operating results and a robust pipeline of opportunities, we're pleased to increase our 2025 investment guidance to approximately $5.0 billion and raise the low-end of our AFFO per share guidance to a range of $4.24 - $4.28.

- Q2 FY25 Press Release, Author's bolded highlight

This is a continuation of the higher opportunities they are seeing particularly in Europe. A lot of the incremental investment activity this year is expected to occur in H2 FY25, with the impact on AFFOs seen more in FY26:

The increase in acquisition volume by $1 billion, as you can tell, it's going to be back-end loaded, second half loaded. And so the impact that one would experience from -- and you correctly said these are all accretive transactions, we wouldn't be doing diluted transactions. The impact of which won't be experienced in 2025, but certainly we'll be -- we'll see the benefits of which in 2026.

- CEO Sumit Roy in the Q2 FY25 earnings call, Author's bolded highlight

Hence, I see a strong case for diluted AFFOs to increase a year from now by around 25%, which is the percentage amount of the incremental increase in investments from $4 billion to $5 billion:

Lower interest rates are a tailwind for the stock

In my last note on Realty Income, my expectations on a vibrant transactions landscape were muted due to views of a higher-for-longer rate environment.

In Q3 FY24, management had remarked that rate cuts can lead to a more attractive transaction landscape. However, it looks like a higher-for-longer rates environment is emerging as the probability of the Fed Funds rate remaining at 425-450bps 2 months from now has increased dramatically from ~50% to almost 86%...

- Hunting Alpha

Now, we are facing the exact opposite scenario. Looking ahead to mid-year next year, the market is pricing in a certainty of Fed rate cuts with a weighted average cut of 99bps from the current band of 400-425bps:

So this too supports the outlook for higher investment activity and portfolio growth going ahead.

O stock is attractively valued at a discount to its comps and may be poised for a multiple re-rating

Another tailwind we can expect from lower rates is P/AFFO multiple expansion from the current 14x levels back toward the long term averages of 17-18x:

And if you look at where we've traded traditionally, we've been right around 17 to 18x is the average multiple of our equity. Today, we trade at 14x. And a lot of this happened primarily because of this high negative correlation that we have with the 10-year [rate]. And so if the expectation is that the 10-year rate is going to start to come down, the expectation is that we will start to revert back to our average multiple, which could then allow us to do more without having to source more.

- CEO Sumit Roy in the 2025 BoFA Global Real Estate Conference, Author's bolded highlight

Currently, O stock's 1-yr fwd P/AFFO is at 14.14x vs. the sector median's 15.31x, corresponding to a 7.64% discount. So valuation is attractive from a relative perspective too.

The technicals are turning bullish

If this is your first time reading a Hunting Alpha article using Technical Analysis, you may want to read this post, which explains how and why I read the charts the way I do. All my charts reflect total shareholder return as they are adjusted for dividends/distributions.

Relative Read of O vs SPX500

Relative to the S&P500, O is now in a range following an incumbent downtrend. I expect this ranging action to continue. In other words, I think we will see more sideways movement and hence performance in-line with the S&P500 going ahead.

Relative Read of O vs CBOE S&P Dividend Aristocrat Index

With a forward dividend yield of 5.33%, O is a member of the CBOE S&P500 Dividend Aristocrat Index (SPAI ticker in TradingView). When we compare against this index, my outlook is more bullish as there are higher lows being printed in the broader accumulation range and good bullish strength in the recent monthly candles.

Management's expectations reflect higher tenant credit risks

Management's commentary suggests increasing tenant credit risk in the overall portfolio. For example, the company's 2025 guidance for rent loss came in higher than historical expectations:

Our 2025 outlook contemplates approximately 75 basis points of potential rent loss, which is slightly higher than our historical experience but consistent with our expectations going into the year. Much of this credit loss is the result of certain tenants acquired through public M&A transactions we have consummated in recent years.

- CEO Sumit Roy in the Q2 FY25 earnings call

Currently, almost 5% of the overall credit portfolio is in the company’s internal watch list that monitors for potential financial stress. Now, the good news is 114 clients make up this cumulative watch list exposure, indicating good diversification of the credit risk. Still, we should take seriously management's caution that harder times may be coming.

...our credit watch list is at 4.6%. But what is very interesting about that credit watch list is we have more than 100 clients who represent that. So on average, each client is circa 4 basis points. So we have diversified that risk as well across a multitude of clients... So yes, well positioned, but I think hard times may come.

- CEO Sumit Roy in the 2025 BoFA Global Real Estate Conference

As for the cause of this increased risk with tenants' creditworthiness, In the Q2 FY25 earnings call, management attributed the elevated credit risk largely to the impact of tariffs stressing some tenant businesses. In terms of financial impact, whilst I do not expect any major write-offs just yet, I would not be surprised if the provisions for rent impairment mix increases in subsequent quarters:

Takeaway & Positioning

I think Realty Income looks good now, especially for yield-focused investors. This year, company is taking advantage of cheaper debt funding rates to capitalize on the higher number of opportunities present in Europe. And encouragingly, all indications are that this trend will continue, supported by a lower interest rate outlook for next year too. Moreover, a dovish interest rate environment also provides a catalyst for a re-rating in the stock, which is already trading at a discount vs sectoral median 1-yr fwd P/AFFOs multiples. The technicals charts also look good especially on O vs the Dividend Aristocrat Index.

A key risk monitorable to the bullish view are credit risks in the overall tenants portfolio. But I do not expect any major rent write-offs at this stage; just watch the provisions for impairment numbers every quarter.

Rating: 'Outperform/Buy'

How to interpret Hunting Alpha's ratings:

Strong Outperform/Strong Buy: Expect the company to outperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis, with higher than usual confidence. I also have a net long position in the security in my personal portfolio.

Outperform/Buy: Expect the company to outperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis

Market Perform/Hold: Expect the company to perform in-line with the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis

Underperform/Sell: Expect the company to underperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis

Strong Underperform/Strong Sell: Expect the company to underperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis, with higher than usual confidence

The typical time-horizon for my views is multiple quarters to more than a year. It is not set in stone. However, I will share updates on my changes in stance in a pinned comment to this article and may also publish a new article discussing the reasons for the change in view.

data.title: Alphabet: Brace For Strong Q3 Earnings
data.description:
Q3 earnings: reasons to be bullish

Alphabet (NASDAQ:GOOGL) (NASDAQ:GOOG) continues demonstrating strong share price momentum with its strong 31% rally over the last three months. Last time I shared my GOOG coverage update was also before the earnings release as the company reported its Q2 financials in late July. In the previous thesis we looked at the company's historical patterns related to earnings revisions and surprises as well as discussed some broader context. This approached worked quite well because the stock is up by 40% since my previous article.

Google's Q3 earnings release is scheduled for October 29 and the company is expected to maintain double-digit revenue growth with analysts estimation of 13.3% YoY growth. This correlates with the growth trend demonstrated by the company over the last several quarters, indicating that AI momentum is still strong. The EPS growth, on the other hand, is expected to be less optimistic with a 7.9% YoY increase. This slight discrepancy between revenue growth and EPS indicates that costs are estimated to increase at a higher rate than revenue. However, expectations for higher costs (e.g. R&D costs) are justifiable, since they are essential to maintain Google's leading positions in the market. Furthermore, given the history of quite high ROIC, these investments in new projects are highly probable to be paid back over the long-term.

Additionally to revenue and EPS growth, earnings revisions are also massively positive, which is another strong argument in favor to be bullish in Q3. Consistent optimism from Wall Street analysts regarding the upcoming earnings release is a good sign, reinforcing overall bullishness as well. Moreover, EPS surprise records have stayed positive over the last ten quarters. Alphabet's quarterly revenue surprise record has also been consistent with only one miss over the last ten quarters. With all that being said about some vital indications before earnings, I expect that Google's EPS and revenue growth will continue to meet/exceed industry's expectations to steadily grow.

If we look beyond just some headline numbers and stats, recent developments add further optimism that reinforces bullish GOOG case. I have already described in detail in my previous GOOG coverages why the company is firmly positioned amid the AI revolution. Therefore, this time I will share my views on news related to the company's positioning in AI.

For example, Anthropic, one of the strongest players in generative AI, recently announced that they will be expanding their partnership with Google. The most impressive part to me is that the partnership expansion is related to using up to a million Google's TPUs, which means "tens of billions of dollars" in additional revenue for GOOG. And it is not about just the money, but about the company's massively expanding AI ecosystem. For many years, Google has been known as a search engine champion supercharged with YouTube as well. I am aware that Google has been working on their TPU for several years and its not quite new. However, the expanded Anthropic deal certainly showcases that their hardware can handle massive enterprise deployments and maybe even challenge Nvidia in supplying AI accelerators for the leading generative AI companies.

Therefore, we see that Alphabet's aggressively growing R&D spending pays off as the company is able to win such large contracts in hardware, an area where we're not quite used to seeing Google at the forefront. And the company is looking even beyond AI era, Google continues aggressively working on their technological edge in quantum computing as well. The company's CEO recently stated that their quantum chip Willow has achieved "the first-ever verifiable quantum advantage", citing that the algorithm (Quantum Echoes) powered by the chip is 13,000x faster than the best classical algorithm on one of the world's fastest supercomputers. Even for a person with no IT background like me this improvement in performance is apparently a big breakthrough. Therefore, Google's technological edge continues expanding far beyond AI as well.

With all its positives, I think that GOOG is very cheaply valued. I see it from the fact that its current TTM P/E ratios are very close to historical averages, meaning that valuation still ignores the robust future revenue and EPS growth potential. As a result, the forward P/E ratio forecast from Wall Street suggests that the FY2028 estimated P/E is just 18.5.

Moreover, another sign that the stock is cheap is the comparison of Alphabet's valuation ratios against other Magnificent 7 stocks. Except for Meta and Google in mid 20-s, all others have the current P/E above 30. Furthermore, GOOGL's valuation ratios related to Sales (Price/Sales, EV/Sales) are also way lower than the other Magnificent 7 representatives. Amazon is an outlier here due to the large portion of its revenues coming from retail operations, which inherently carry lower valuations.

Reasons to be bearish

Despite attractive valuation from the perspective of TTM and forward P/E ratios, Wall Street's average target price of $255 is very close to the current market value.

This indicates that analysts see very limited upside potential from current levels. I think that their skepticism is mostly based on the fact that while Google's Gemini generative AI offering is accumulating momentum, it will not create additional value for shareholders but will cannibalize financial success of the search engine. While this might be a reasonable fear because generative AI is used for search as well, I think that the fear might be a little bit exaggerated on a consolidated basis.

The reason why I think so is because Google Workspace has already become a large player in office applications. With that being said, even if generative AI cannibalizes some part of the Search's financial success, cross-selling AI features to Google Workspace users has great potential to add additional value for shareholders.

I think that the earnings-specific risk is GOOGL's quite high RSI indicator of almost 61. This is close to the overbought area, which might mean that expectations are already quite high before the upcoming earnings release. Therefore, even if the company delivers a solid double beat against consensus together with upbeat guidance, the market's reaction might be mild.

The verdict

I think that GOOG remains significantly undervalued prior to the company's Q3 earnings release, which is unfair given all the bullish catalysts in motion. Therefore, I maintain Strong Buy for the stock.

data.title: SPY: Brace For Recessionary Bear Market With A Bubble Burst
data.description:
The Bubble continues to inflate

This is my quarterly update on the S&P500 (NYSEARCA:SPY) targeted for longer term investors. The last update in July highlighted the bubble risk and noted that the S&P 500 could continue to rise over the near term only if the PE multiple expands - meaning if the bubble continues to inflate.

In July, the Shiller PE ratio was 37, now it's over 40, which is an 8% multiple expansion to the near 2000-dot-com levels highs, while the S&P500 is 9% higher since then. So, over the last three months, the bubble continued to inflate.

In the same sense, the S&P 500 could continue to rise over the next three months if the bubble continues to inflate. However, for longer term investors this is not a reason to hold or to buy - the bubble will eventually burst, and this could happen randomly as the sentiment suddenly shifts.

The bubble burst could be triggered by a recession, and in this article, I will show that the likelihood of an imminent recession is very high.

Thus, due to the possibility of a deep selloff on par with 2000 and 2008 bear markets, my rating for SPY continues to be Strong Sell.

An imminent recession

The key point from my July report was that the bubble could burst due to stagflation. In fact, stagflation continues to be the current state, but based on the recent data, it's likely to transition to an imminent recession. I talked about all the variables that are currently producing stagflation, so here is the summary table.

Variable #

Description

GDP

Labor Market

Inflation

3

Student loan payments

0

0

5

AI-related job losses

Neg

Neg

0

Neg

Neg

Neg

Positive

Positive

Based on the net effect of all of these variables, my current outlook is stagflation, likely to transition to a recession:

  • GDP: Significant downside risk
  • Labor Market: Weakening
  • Inflation: Sticky

This outlook is not much different from what the Fed is predicting in the SEP, although, the Fed does not predict a recession - the market would panic, but the fact is the Fed is lowering interest rates due to a weakening labor market, and that precedes a recession.

Predicted outlook alone is not helpful, unless it's supported by data. So, here is the recent soft data:

University of Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index

55.0

Weak confidence due to high prices and job concerns

Matches weak labor market and GDP outlook

Consumer Expectations Index (U. Michigan)

51.2

Pessimism about future finances and job prospects

Matches labor market weakness

Inflation Expectations (1-Year, U. Michigan)

4.6%

Down slightly, but still elevated

Matches inflation pressure from tariffs

Inflation Expectations (5-Year, U. Michigan)

3.7%

Sticky long-run inflation expectations

Matches persistent inflation outlook

NY Fed Survey – 1-Year Inflation Expectations

3.4%

Rising food and rent inflation expectations

Confirms inflationary pressures

NY Fed Survey – Job Loss Probability

14.9%

Above 12-month average, labor market stress rising

Matches labor market weakness

NY Fed Survey – Unemployment Expectations

41.1%

Highest since early 2023

Strong confirmation of labor market risk

S&P Global Manufacturing PMI

52.0

Slight decline, new orders slowing due to tariffs

Matches slowing GDP from trade policy

ISM Manufacturing PMI

49.1

Still in contraction, employment index weak

Matches GDP and labor market weakness

S&P Global Services PMI

53.9

Slight decline, still in expansion

Moderately strong ***

Conference Board Consumer Confidence Index

94.2

Five-month low, expectations index below recession threshold

Matches recession risk outlook

Thus, soft-data confirms the macro-outlook - it points to stagflation, with more downside risk for growth. Only the PMI Service still shows some strength.

However, the soft data also has to be confirmed by hard data, for the outlook to be relevant. So, here is the recent hard data:

GDP Growth

2.3% (median nowcast)

H1 GDP ex AI related capex only 0.1%

Growth slowing from Q2’s 3.8%, heavily influenced by AI

Consistent if exclude AI

Unemployment Rate

4.3% (Aug est.)

Highest since 2021, job creation stalled, layoffs rising

Matches labor market weakness

ADP Private Employment

-32,000 jobs (Sep)

Hiring at lowest since 2009

Strong confirmation

Chicago Fed Labor Indicators

Hiring rate: 45.2%; Layoffs: 2.1%; Unemployment forecast: 4.34%

Hiring slowing, layoffs steady, unemployment creeping up

Matches labor deterioration

CPI Inflation

3.1% Y/Y; Core CPI: 3.1%

Inflation driven by tariffs, shelter and food costs elevated

Matches inflation pressure

PCE Inflation (Aug)

2.7% headline; 2.9% core

Tariffs added 0.4–0.5% to inflation, based on Fed study

Matches inflation outlook

Retail Sales (Aug)

+0.6% M/M; +5.0% YoY

Strong nominal growth, real growth more modest

Slightly stronger than GDP outlook****

Housing Starts (Aug)

1.307M units, -8.5% M/M

Weakest since May 2020, multi-family down sharply

Matches GDP and labor

Hard data also confirms the macro-outlook and matches soft data. Only retail sales are still strong, in addition GDP that includes AI related capex.

Thus, we are likely facing an imminent recession, which is consistent with 1) current policies in place, 2) recent soft data, and 3) recent hard data.

Cross-asset confirmation

All predictions are useless, unless there is some kind of confirmation from the asset prices, bonds particularly. Bond market is usually the most efficient in pricing the macro situation.

The 2-year Treasury Note (US2Y) and the Federal Funds futures are already pricing a more agressive interest rate easing policy, consistent with the recession. Currently, the Federal Funds futures are pricing the Federal funds rate at 2.9% in December 2026, below the Fed's 3.4% prediction.

The 10-Year yield (US10Y) has also been falling, even below 4%, with real yields falling and inflation expectations falling, and this is also consistent with a significant economic slowdown.

Cyclical commodities have also been falling, led by crude oil (USO), which signals a global recession. This is also confirmed by the strengthening US dollar (DXY), and ultimately rising gold (GLD).

The SP500 sectors

So, what led the S&P500 higher by 6% over the last three months? It was Technology (XLK) up by 11%. The AI trade broadly includes Communications (XLC), and Discretionary (XLY), this is where Mag 7 stocks are other than Tech, and also Utilities (XLU) - all performed strongly. Thus, it was all about the AI trade, and that's exactly where the bubble is.

Implications

The S&P500 is a bubble, based on many measures such as the Shiller PE ratio. This bubble is facing a recession, as I explain in this article. Thus, investors can expect that the bubble will burst as the recession becomes more evident in the hard data, which could be imminent, over the next few months. I would not wait to sell, as bubbles could burst randomly.

data.title: SPYI Remains Attractive In A Yield Starved Environment
data.description:
Over the years covered-call ETFs have become a popular option for income investors looking to exceed the risk-free rate of return. More and more investment houses have launched option-based products with their own take on the premise of selling covered calls to generate income which is disbursed to shareholders in addition to the dividend income which is collected from the underlying holdings. Global X pioneered these funds with their suite of covered call ETFs that started back in 2013 and over the years everyone from JPMorgan Asset Management to Goldman Sachs (GS) has entered the space. Each product is unique but the concept is the same. Investors who are allocating capital toward covered-call ETFs are looking for extra income and are not necessarily worried about replicating the returns of the market. There has been a clash between growth investors who view the investment realm as a vehicle to maximize returns while there is an entire segment of the market that is focused on yield and generating recurring income. The Neos S&P 500 High Income ETF (BATS:SPYI) has been one of my favorite ETFs from an income perspective and it remains very attractive in my opinion as we enter a market that is become yield starved. SPYI was established in September of 2022 and since then shares have only appreciated by 6.29% but they have generated $18.89 of income which works out to an annualized yield of 12.47%. I believe that the market is positioned to go higher and that SPYI is attractive at these levels as it is structured to continue generating recurring income that far exceeds the risk-free rate of return in an environment where the Fed is lowering rates.

Following up on my previous article about SPYI

At the beginning of August I wrote an article on SPYI (can be read here) and discussed why I was still very bullish on it as an income producing asset. SPYI has increased by 1.54% since the article was published and the S&P 500 has increased by 5.33%. SPYI is not structured to outperform the market but the key statistic here is that it’s not sacrificing upside for yield. SPYI is structured to appreciate to some degree as the market goes higher while paying out large amounts of income. SPYI has generated a total return of 4.67% since then when the distributions are accounted for. I had discussed how its dual-leg option strategy delivers double-digit yields without sacrificing all upside and allows it to outperform some of its popular peers. I felt that we were headed into a easing period of monetary policy and it would make SPYI more popular as bonds and traditional risk-free investments wouldn’t be able to sustain their previous levels of yield that made the phrase T-bill and chill popular. I am following up with a new article to discuss how SPYI is performing now that the Fed started cutting rates, how it compares to it’s peers, and why I think it will become more popular as we enter a yield starved environment.

How NEOS Investments created a more solid covered call ETF with SPYI

Global X will always be looked at as the pioneer in the covered-call ETF space as their S&P 500 Covered-Call ETF (XYLD) was launched in 2013. Since then shares of XYLD have remained basically flat as they are down -0.09% at a time when investing in a simple index fund such as the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) has increased by 222.88%. XYLD has sacrificed all the upside for recurring income and paid their shareholders $38.31 of distribution income since inception which is a 95.77% of the initial share price. There is nothing wrong with this and from an income perspective investors have been able to average around a 7.7% yield through XYLD which significantly exceeds the risk-free rate of return but at the end of the day investors have sacrificed a lot of upside not average below 10%.

NEOS Investments found a way to keep a respectable pace with the total return from the S&P 500 through their version of a covered-call ETF. Over the past year SPYI has generated a 15% total return while SPY has generated a 16% return. Unlike other ETFs that just focus on writing covered calls at the money to generate income at the expense of capping future upside potential, SPYI generates recurring high monthly income from an actively managed portfolio that deploys a 2-leg call option strategy. SPYI will write call options in the first leg on its underlying positions to generate income but where they differ from other ETF’s is a portion of that income is retained and used to purchase out of the money call options. This allows SPYI to participate in some of the upside in a market that is appreciating.

SPYI's holdings mimic the S&P 500 with the Magnificent Seven representing most of the top-10 positions. SPYI's option overlay strategy is significantly different than its peers as it allows investors to participate in growth from the largest companies in the market through its 2-leg strategy. Since inception SPYI has generated $18.89 of distributed income at a pretty even monthly rate. This has allowed SPYI to achieve an average annualized yield that exceeds 12% as the premium received from the call options creates a net credit where the premium received from writing option contracts exceeds the cost of buying call options. By utilizing this strategy SPYI has been able to protect some of the upside by owing out of the money call options and generate a large monthly distribution from the dividends collected and remaining option premiums that have been generated.

SPYI is generating a larger yield in 2025 than it’s peers and I think this will continue

In the table below I have compared SPYI to SPY which is my baseline for the market, XYLD, the JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPI), and the Goldman Sachs S&P 500 Premium Income ETF (GPIX). XYLD focuses on writing covered calls at the money which caps almost all of the upside potential while JEPI has a different strategy as it runs it’s option strategy in the exchange linked note (ELN) section of its portfolio. The ELNs are derivatives that are designed to combine the characteristics of the S&P 500 and written call options in a single note. This allows JEPI to produce some appreciation unlike XYLD. Goldman Sachs entered the space with GPIX which utilizes a dynamic options overwrite strategy to generate its monthly distribution income. GPIX will write call options on a varying percentage of the market value of its equity portfolio to produce immediate premiums. I have compiled a table with the metrics from 2025 below and the data indicates that SPYI is the only ETF that has already generated a double digit yield on cost if you had purchased shares at the beginning of the year while having a positive ROI on the underlying equity.

I went through the data for 2025 which consisted of where the share prices closed on 12/31/24, how much income has been generated throughout 2025, and what the total return has been compared to the market for SPYI, GPIX, JEPI, and XYLD. The results are interesting as SPY has generated 14.46% of appreciation and 0.90% in dividend income for a total return of 15.37% as my baseline. None of these ETFs have exceeded the total return from SPY but I am not expecting them to. XYLD has fallen -5.35% and generated 8.46% in distributions for a total return of 3.12%. JEPI is similar as it has -0.24% in appreciation and generated 6.22% for a total return of 5.97%. In all fairness JEPI hasn’t declared the October distribution to I would consider the total return to be between 6.5% and 7% once it is paid. GPIX is the big winner for capital appreciation as it’s strategy has generated a return of 5.84% while it has produced another 7.04% from its distribution. GPIX has slightly outpaced all of the ETFs in total return with 12.88% compared to 12.72% from SPYI. SPYI has produced 10.02% of distributed income from the share price at the beginning of the year while also gaining 2.7% of appreciation. It’s total return is 12.72% while already producing a double digit yield on cost.

When I look at the 3 years worth of data from SPYI it has averaged a monthly distribution of $0.50 per share. This is roughly $6 of annualized income. With a share price of $52.19 this would work out to an 11.5% annualized yield. The lowest monthly distribution has been just over $0.46 and if I use this as the baseline for the next year SPYI would generate $5.54 of income which would be a forward yield of 10.61%. I feel good about SPYI generating a double digit yield going forward on an annualized basis and its doing so while providing some capital appreciation from its strategy. I think that SPYI will continue to be a winner in the space as they are closing in on $6 billion in assets under management (AUM).

Conclusion and why I am still excited for SPYI

CME Group is projecting that there is a 98.9% chance that we get a 25 bps reduction at the October Fed meeting. The Fed Dot Plot indicates that the most likely scenario is that rates end up around 3.5% at the end of 2026 and 3% at the end of 2027. There is still more than $7.48 trillion sitting in money market accounts according to the ST. Louis Fed which doesn’t include CD’s or bonds. Currently all bonds from the 3-Month to the 10-Year have a 4% yield or lower and that is likely to continue declining over the next 2-years. I believe that the risk-free asset movement that investors flocked to will crack over the next year and a portion of this capital will flow back into the equity markets. This will likely be very bullish for SPYI as I believe investors will look to its strong yield which has established a track record of more than 3-years and as new place to park capital to yield farm. SPYI is becoming the best of both worlds for income investors as it allows them to participate to some degree in the direction of the market while generating recurring monthly income with an annualized yield that is averaging in the double digits. I am very bullish on SPYI going forward and will be adding more to my position.

data.title: ONEOK: Fee-Based Stability Amid AI Frenzy
data.description:
My Thesis

As the AI craze continues, I am seeking high-quality opportunities to expand the weight of my defensive stocks. Readers who have followed me for a long time know that I like investing in large midstream companies because of their financial stability and lower dependence of their earnings on the prices of hydrocarbons. As I plan to expand part of my portfolio invested in midstream assets, I want to diversify and invest in a new company for me, which is ONEOK, Inc. (NYSE:OKE). It is not as popular and large as industry leaders like Enbridge or Energy Transfer, but it still offers a compelling forward dividend yield of 6% and solid upside potential. Natural gas exposure is solid, which is crucial because of the expected crucial role of this fuel type to drive AI innovation. Capital allocation looks sound, meaning that OKE will highly likely be able to balance generous dividend payouts together with investments in future growth. All in all, I initiate OKE coverage with a "Strong Buy" rating.

OKE Analysis

Since it is my first coverage of ONEOK, I start with a brief description of the company's business. According to the FY2024 10-K report, OKE is a "leading midstream service provider of gathering, processing, fractionation, transportation, storage and marine export services". The management says that 90% of the company's consolidated earnings were fee-based in 2024. It is very good because it means limited dependence on highly volatile commodity prices. ONEOK operates via four segments, which are:

  • Natural Gas Gathering and Processing;
  • Natural Gas Liquids [NGL];
  • Natural Gas Pipelines; and
  • Refined Products and Crude.

The business mix looks robust because it is not about all eggs in the same basket in ONEOK's case. Yes, the NGL segment is by far the largest one both in terms of revenue and adjusted EBITDA. However, the Natural Gas Gathering and Processing segment as well as the Refined Products and Crude Segments are also the ones that generate billions per year both in revenue and EBITDA. The company boasts a 60,000-mile pipeline network and loads of other facilities strategically positioned in America's most prolific oil and gas basins. It is not easy to replicate such a vast infrastructure, which ensures OKE's wide strategic moat. Therefore, we see that OKE's strategic positioning is strong. Moreover, the company is rebalancing its portfolio of assets to maximize value for shareholders.

And the management converts this strong strategic positioning into financial excellence. OKE demonstrated double-digit CAGR over the last decade across the board of the most important profitability metrics. I also like that OKE's profitability ratios outpaced revenue growth over the last decade. To me it certainly indicates the management's value to maximize value for shareholders via cost efficiency.

The upcoming quarter's earnings are scheduled for October 28 post-market. OKE's Q3 revenue is expected to be $8.23 billion, which will be higher by 4% if compared on a QoQ basis. The best part is that the company's bottom line is expected to outpace revenue growth with a projected 7% QoQ increase, from $1.34 to $1.44. I am not comparing on a YoY basis because there will be huge percentage changes due to the effect of M&A spree during late 2024 and early 2025. But the main point here is that OKE continues demonstrating revenue growth and operating leverage, which are the most important criteria for me as a value investor.

Another crucial point for any value investor is a company's financial position. Midstream expansion projects are costly and usually have long durations, meaning that companies are raising large amounts via debt financing. Therefore, I am not surprised by OKE's large $32.5 billion total debt. With the company's strong FCF generating capacity, the covered ratio sits at a comfortable level of 3.3. Moreover, OKE's total debt is significantly lower compared to the market cap, meaning there is solid room for debt restructuring if needed.

I expect the company to maintain its robust balance sheet over the long term and the management to remain confident in its FY2025 outlook. Despite volatility in oil prices, OKE is likely to benefit from broader favorable trends like the current global data center spending spree as well as the strategic priority of President Trump's administration to fortify the manufacturing potential of the country. Both data centers and new factories will need uninterrupted energy supply, and a player with an extensive midstream ecosystem will certainly be there to ensure that energy transfers from oil and gas basins to end customers.

Valuation

To value a dividend machine like OKE, I usually prefer the approach called the dividend discount model [DDM]. The discount rate is 7.54%, which is the company's cost of equity calculated using the CAPM approach.

Wall Street analysts forecast OKE's FY2026 dividend to be $4.30. To be conservative, a 2% perpetual growth rate was used, which aligns with the Fed's inflation target.

According to my calculations, OKE's fair share price is almost $78. The last close is around $69, meaning that there is an attractive 13% upside potential. The upside looks especially good if you also keep in mind OKE's 6% forward dividend yield.

Wall Street analysts are even more optimistic regarding the fair share price is their average target price is far above my estimates. The consensus target price of $92.5 means that Wall Street analysts believe that the stock is 34% undervalued. From the forward P/E ratios forecast's perspective, OKE is also extremely undervalued with the expected decline of P/E from around 13 to below 10 over the next few years.

Risks To Consider

The business faces significant operational risks that might lead to material, unfavorable financial and reputational consequences. Dangers include pipeline leaks, equipment failures, and disruptions due to weather events or natural disasters.

As I mentioned at the beginning of my analysis, OKE's revenues are mostly fee-based, meaning protection from volatility in crude oil or natural gas prices. However, they are vulnerable to unfavorable changes in the energy supply-demand equilibrium. Weaker production activity in the energy upstream sector will inevitably lead to less throughput volumes on OKE's systems.

Now let's also mention the risks related to the upcoming earnings release. ONEOK's earnings surprise record is quite far from perfect with six negative revenue and five EPS misses over the last eight quarters. When a company delivers a solid double beat against consensus forecasts, there is a quite high probability of the share price rising significantly because of the growing investors' confidence. And vice versa, when the business struggles to consistently surpass consensus estimates, investors' confidence melts down. In case of a new double miss, the Q3 earnings release might become a negative stock price catalyst.

Bottom Line

To conclude, OKE is a "Strong Buy". I choose the stock of its quality and strategic positioning in the rock-solid midstream industry of the United States. Compelling dividend yield and notable undervaluation are likely to protect me in case of the broader stock market's deteriorating sentiment.

data.title: Crispr And Intellia: Advancing In Vivo Gene Editing As 2025 Results Strengthen Investment Outlook
data.description:
Background

My last coverage of Crispr Therapeutics (NASDAQ:CRSP) and Intellia Therapeutics (NASDAQ:NTLA) was published in May 2025. At the time, gene therapy stocks were suffering from regulatory uncertainties, potential pricing regulations in the U.S., and distrust given rising safety and commercialisation concerns. As stated in my previous article, the pipeline and financial stability of both stocks supported a bullish case for CRSP and NTLA, which were trading at $38.25 and $8.84, respectively. Although the uncertainties surrounding gene therapy stocks are not completely gone, in the recent months the market has been getting more optimistic about the therapeutic potential of in vivo gene therapies, and therefore its commercialisation potential. This market optimism triggered both stocks to outperform when compared against SP500’s performance in the last six-months (see image below).

NTLA had the best performance, rewarding investors with 241% total returns in the last six-months. Given that Intellia is still in a pre-commercialisation stage, expecting to reach commercial stage in 2027, its investment thesis has a higher risk profile than the investment in CRSP, which has a larger cash runway, a diversified pipeline, and a commercial product (CASGEVY). Demonstrating that sometimes investing in companies with a high-risk value translates into high returns. But, past performance does not guarantee future results.

The current analysis will focus on the in vivo gene therapies developed by CRSP and NTLA, as in my opinion those product candidates have the largest potential to boost investment returns.

Thesis

Crispr is expected to release the Phase I clinical trial results from CTX310 before yearend. CTX310 is an in vivo single dose gene therapy aiming to delete angiopoietin-like 3 (ANGPTL3) for the treatment of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (ASCVD). The company is estimating that in the U.S. alone, there are more than 40 million patients that could benefit from CTX310 if/when commercially available. ASCVD’s total addressable market in 2024 was estimated at $24 billion across the seven largest markets, and it is projected to reach up to $32.3 billion by 2035. Beyond this product candidate, CASGEVY is projected to deliver increasing revenue, which is expected to push Crispr’s balance sheet to break even from 2026. While other promising product candidates including allogeneic CAR-Ts targeting cancer and autoimmune disease complete a diverse pipeline that support a bullish case for long-term investors. However, taking in consideration the current valuation, and the binary nature of CTX310 upcoming clinical trial results, I downgrade my rating for CRSP from “Strong Buy” to a “Hold” as I believe the share price is already pricing in the potential success of CTX310 in Phase I trials.

Intellia, on the other hand, has a smaller pipeline solely focused on in vivo gene therapies. The company is expecting to file for regulatory approval of Lonvo-z (previously known as NTLA-2002) in the second half of 2026. If approved, Lonvo-z will become Intellia’s first commercial asset. This product candidate is aimed as a single dose in vivo gene therapy for the treatment of Hereditary Angioedema (HAE). Recently, Ionis Pharmaceuticals (IONS) gained FDA approval for Dawnzera, an RNA-based treatment against HAE. In contrast with Lonvo-z, which is a single dose gene therapy, Ionis’ RNA-based treatment was approved as once every four and once every eight weeks prophylactic therapy against HAE. Since Dawnzera’s approval, IONS share price rose 67%, currently trading at $71/sh. Although Lonvo-z may have the therapeutic advantage against Dawnzera, as a single dose treatment, its potential approval is forecasted for H1 2027 at the earliest. The HAE total addressable market in 2025 was estimated at $3.13 billion, and it is expected to be valued at $5.96 billion by 2032. Taking in consideration the low competitive landscape in HAE, Lonvo-z’s potential regulatory success is poised to boost NTLA’s share price in the next twelve months. Thus, I reiterate my “Buy” rating for long-term investors with a high risk tolerance, while recommending caution and diversification given the binary nature of such an investment thesis based on potential regulatory outcomes.

In vivo gene editing

The field of gene therapies is currently evolving towards a very exciting future. The treatments based on gene therapies (including gene editing, RNA-based, DNA-based) were focused on rare conditions, partially because the patients suffering from those conditions have a lack of efficient therapeutic options, thus justifying the development of precision medicines. Two very well-known examples are CASGEVY and ELEVIDYS, for the treatment of sickle-cell disease and Duchenne muscular dystrophy, respectively. However, some biotech and pharmaceutical companies are moving towards the development of gene therapies targeting conditions with a larger patient population, for instance Voyager Therapeutics (VYGR) has gene therapy product candidates targeting Alzheimer’s disease, Parkinson’s disease, among others. This technology is currently being developed for the treatment of cancer, autoimmune disease, neurodegenerative and cardiovascular conditions.

In the area of cardiovascular, the product candidates from Crispr and Intellia are particularly exciting given their use of non-viral vectors with high tissue specificity and their capacity to be therapeutically efficient after a single intravenous dose. If successful in clinical trials through regulatory approval, I believe this technology could be transformative in the treatment of cardiovascular conditions, which remain one of the largest causes of death worldwide.

Crispr Therapeutics

Crispr accounts with four gene editing in vivo programs, all of them aimed to be delivered via intravenous systemic injection. The product candidates consist of a lipid nanoparticle that encapsulates a Cas9 mRNA with a guide RNA targeting the gene of interest. The two most advanced programs are CTX310 and CTX320, which are in Phase I clinical trials (see image below).

CTX310 and CTX320 are targeting indications in ASCVD. CTX310 is expected to deliver Phase I results before the year-end. To date, the preliminary results have been very promising, showing over 81% reduction in triglycerides and up to 64.6% reduction in LDL cholesterol after 30 days of treatment (see image below), without presenting any safety or tolerability concerns.

Given the impressive topline results, the market is counting with positive Phase I results, supporting the progression of this product candidate towards Phase II. However, CTX310’s potential positive results are likely to validate Crispr’s in vivo gene editing platform, which includes CTX320, CTX340 and CTX450. In my previous analysis, I highlighted the therapeutic and financial advantages of the in vivo gene editing platforms against the ex vivo therapies, i.e. CASGEVY. Briefly, the in vivo gene editing platform offers the opportunity to be manufactured in bulk, to be delivered to the patient as off-the-shelf treatment, in a safe and timely manner, while also reducing COGS significantly, and therefore increasing the profit margins. Furthermore, Crispr’s in vivo gene editing platform is targeting indications with large patient populations with the potential to become large revenue makers if/when they gain regulatory approval.

Intellia Therapeutics

Intellia’s whole pipeline is focused on two single-dose in vivo gene editing product candidates. Lonvo-z against HAE and Nex-z for the treatment of Transthyretin Amyloidosis (ATTR). The first one is expected to become commercially available by 2027, pending regulatory approval. While Nex-z is projected to reach commercial stage by 2028 (see image below).

Based on Intellia’s projections, both product candidates have blockbuster potential by 2028. Likewise, Crispr’s in vivo gene editing candidates, Intellia’s candidates utilise lipid nanoparticles to encapsulate the gene editing molecules. The use of lipid nanoparticles with specific targeting not only promote the efficient intracellular delivery of therapeutic molecules, but also enable the scalability of manufacturing procedures that in turn reduces COGS, when compared against viral delivery, and facilitates the stock availability to cover the potential commercial demand.

Lonvo-z’s phase II long-term results revealed that 98% of patients treated with the product candidates were free from HEA attacks after 3 years of infusion. While 73% of them were also free from using any other treatment to control their condition. The strong phase II clinical trial performance resulted in a positive opinion from prescribers that supports strong commercial potential of the treatment if/when it becomes commercially available (see image below).

Financials

Crispr Therapeutics and Intellia Therapeutics are due to release Q3 2025 financial results on November 6th. Generally speaking, I am not expecting any major surprises from the financial point of view, although Crispr has a commercially available product, the management seems to be waiting for the Q4 2025 report to release CASGEVY’s full year revenue performance, while their large cash availability allows them to fund operations, including a diverse pipeline in various development stages. On the other hand, Intellia remains in clinical-stage, while their cash availability is significantly smaller when compared against Crispr’s, their pipeline is focused on two assets with the potential to become commercially available in 2027 and 2028. The following table summarises the financial performance of both companies as stated in their H1 2025 results.

Despite holding over $1.7 billion, last week, Crispr filed a $600 million stock offer which aims to secure cash at the company’s discretion. Crispr’s management is avoiding providing specific guidance associated with CASGEVY’s full year sales revenue. However, in its quarterly reports, and recent conference presentations the management sounds confident about CASGEVY’s capacity to drive the company towards profitability by 2028, while supporting the development of multiple promising assets, including the in vivo gene editing platform. In terms of CASGEVY roll-out, Vertex Pharmaceuticals (VRTX), who is CASGEVY’s co-developer, has already activated over 75 authorised treatment centres worldwide, while over 115 patients have performed the cell collection stage associated with the treatment. For more details, check out my first analysis of Crispr where I described CASGEVY’s treatment journey. Overall, if I estimate Crispr’s product revenue as zero, the cash on hand ($1.721 billion) equates to a cash runway of 2.2 years at an average quarterly burn rate of $189.5 million. This cash runway will enable Crispr to advance several of their product candidates, including the allogeneic CAR-Ts and in vivo gene editing candidates, which are all aimed as off-the-shelf products, with lower COGS and higher profit margins when compared against CASGEVY. Thus, from the financial point of view, the company is very healthy and able to support its promising pipeline.

On the other hand, Intellia is being very open with its revenue expectations, presuming regulatory approval, they expect their assets to be commercially successful from the first year of launch. Considering that the in vivo gene editing therapies are aimed as off-the-shelf products which have a reduced manufacturing complexity, and lower COGS when compared against ex-vivo therapies, and more importantly the positive opinion of prescribers who appreciate the therapeutic advantage of Lonvo-z and Nex-z, I agree with Intellia’s optimistic revenue guidance from 2027 onwards. The company accounts for $459 million in cash, which considering the average quarterly burn rate in H1 2025 ($130.5 million) equates to 3.5 quarters cash runway (approximately Q4 2026). Nonetheless, Intellia is estimating its cash position is sufficient to cover operations until early 2027, which might be possible presuming they will reduce operating expenses when approaching Lonvo-z clinical trial completion. In this sense, NTLA’s financial position is relatively stable but dependent on clinical and regulatory success of their product candidates.

Valuation

CRSP is currently trading at $67.21 per share, and $6.11 billion market cap. Generally speaking, this year, Crispr has provided significant advances in its pipeline, taking advantage of their financial stability to fund the development of the next generation of assets. In the mid- to long-term, Crispr has the potential to follow the steps of their partner Vertex, in becoming an established profitable biotech/biopharmaceutical company with high quality therapeutic assets. Hence, I believe Crispr will be able to achieve profitability by 2028 at the latest, primarily supported by CASGEVY but being closely followed by other products, including those derived from the in vivo gene editing platform. Despite my long-term bullish sentiment, in this article I am providing a “Hold” rating on CRSP, a rating downgrade from “Strong Buy” six months ago. The main rationale behind the downgrade is that I believe the market has already priced in the potential Phase I clinical success of CTX310. Currently, Wall Street analysts are still given a Buy rating to CRSP, with an average share price target at $82.95. Meanwhile, the Seeking Alpha analyst consensus and Quant rating are rating the stock with a hold. Overall, the EPS growth is expected to be negative in 2025, while observing moderate growth in 2026 onwards, which supports my cautious assessment at this moment in time. Furthermore, insider trades are revealing the recent sell of common stock.

Intellia’s shares are trading at $25.89, and a $2.78 billion market cap. The company has gone through a series of changes this year that have secured their financial stability and business competitiveness. Lonvo-z and Nex-z clinical progress has helped Intellia to regain the market’s trust and positive opinion. For instance, Cathie Wood’s ARK fund has recently increased their position in NTLA and CRSP, while Citizens JMP raised their NTLA’s price target to $33/sh based on their HAE product candidate. This year, the consensus projects the EPS to grow by 21%, while observing a 3.44% growth in 2026 and 61% EPS growth in 2027. Projections based on Lonvo-z’s positive clinical and commercial outcome. In terms of analyst sentiment, NTLA is holding buy ratings from Wall Street ($34 average share price target), Seeking Alpha analysts and the Quant rating. Based on the clinical data released to date, I am optimistic about Lonvo-z potential to achieve commercial status by 2027, while the high clinical need and low competitive pressure supports the revenue projections suggesting commercial success from the first year of launch. In this sense, I reiterate NTLA’s moderate “Buy” rating for long-term investors with a high risk tolerant portfolio, while recommending implementing risk mitigating strategies such as diversification.

Risks

CRSP and NTLA have observed significant share price increases year-to-date, while their business and clinical performance supports the market optimism, it is also true that their investment thesis is based on speculative assumptions associated with the clinical and commercial success of high-risk product candidates.

In my opinion, Crispr, at this moment in time, is less risky than NTLA as an investment opportunity. But, NTLA’s mature in vivo gene editing product candidates and low competitive landscape has a larger potential to boost investor’s returns faster. Hence, my reiterated buy rating. However, investors interested in NTLA must consider the high risk of such speculative investment thesis based on potential clinical and commercial performance. Although the Phase Lonvo-z’ Phase II results were pretty impressive, given the relatively low number of participants, it is possible that the results do not translate into clinical trial success at Phase III.

Furthermore, the multiple regulatory and macroeconomic headwinds highlighted in my previous article focused on CRSP and NTLA are still considerable risks that may have a negative impact on profitability and share price of several biopharmaceutical and biotech stocks, including Crispr and Intellia.

Conclusions

In summary, 2025 has been a successful year for Crispr and Intellia. Both companies have sailed through multiple headwinds, while still supporting the development of product candidates with the potential, e.g. in vivo gene editing candidates, to become blockbusters in the next 5 years. The following table provides a summary of the upcoming catalysts of both companies (See image below)

In my opinion, Crispr is on a positive trajectory that supports their evolution towards becoming profitable by 2028 or earlier. However, in the short-term, I believe the share price already reflects the potential positive performance of CTX310 and the successful pipeline developments achieved in 2025. Thus, supporting my temporary “Hold” rating.

Likewise, Intellia has observed a sharp share price increase in the last six months. Mostly supported by pipeline developments, as well as positive sentiment about the company’s business decisions earlier this year. Intellia accounts for enough cash and equivalents to support operations until observing the clinical outcome of Lonvo-z in HEA. If the asset achieves clinical and regulatory success, Intellia’s shares are likely to observe a sharp increase similar to the one observed by IONS. However, the opposite scenario may cause a significant sell-off. While I reiterate my “Buy” rating for NTLA. Should investors start or increase positions in NTLA, they should also consider the binary nature of such speculative investment thesis and apply risk mitigating strategies such as diversification.

data.title: Molina Healthcare: Looks Like A Sick Puppy
data.description:
Molina Healthcare (NYSE:MOH) is an upstart in the Health Insurance space. I last wrote about Molina in an article comparing them to United Healthcare (UNH) the old dog in the space.

At the time, I concluded that Molina probably offered the better valuation of the pair, and faced similar risks, however without the scale benefits of UNH.

Molina came out with earnings on October 22nd, and the results were terrible.

The company slashed guidance, cutting EPS estimates for the current year from $19 to $14. The problem was continued medical costs inflation. While much of the weakness in the Marketplace segment, this only accounted for half of the deterioration.

The continued slide in margins significantly reduces the credibility of the Molina outlook.

The market seems to agree with this. As at the time of writing, the Molina share price had dropped over 15%. Peer Centene (CNC) had dropped in sympathy by 4%. Elevance (ELV) had fared better, having reported Q3 already, while United Healthcare (UNH), also due to report, escaped pretty much unscathed.

Q3 Earnings and Outlook

Some level of negative response was inevitable following the big haircut in guidance. The quarter had some ominous figures.

Premium revenues were up around 11% year on year. However earnings per share shrunk to $1,51 compared to $5.65 for the comparable period, on a net income of $79m, down from $326m year on year.

The big driver of the miss was the medical care ratio, which came in at 92.6%, which shaved the after tax margin to a barely profitable 0.7% of revenues.

The company reported pressure in the medical care ratio in all segments, while pointing out that half of the deterioration came from the Marketplace segment, which accounted for just 10% of revenues. The MCR shift was dramatic from 85.4% to 95.6% for this segment.

This seems to be an attempt to pitch the problem as more easily addressable, by strong action in that one segment. However, the broad nature of the declines hints at a more pronounced utilisation, medical inflation, and pricing problem.

Of further concern is a negative cash flow print. Operating cash flow for the year to date was a negative of $237 million in contrast to the strong cash flow generation of $868m for the first 9 months of 2025. This is not due to cash being invested, but rather normal claims and Medicaid risk payments.
